title: Stocks recover ground after early dive
full_text:
The sharemarket sensationally recovered ground on Tuesday, following a “stomach churning” fall at the opening bell.
The benchmark S&P/ASX 200 closed down 20.9 points, or 0.24 per cent, at 8604.2, while the broader All Ordinaries also finished in the red, down 31.1 points, or 0.35 per cent, at 8824.8.
While both indices traded lower, it was a strong recovery throughout the afternoon.
The dollar added 0.25 per cent against the greenback to be buying US70.54c. Overall, seven of the 11 sectors finished higher, with a drop in materials offsetting gains out of telecommunications, consumer and healthcare stocks.
Telstra jumped 2.21 per cent to $5.08, TPG added 1.1 per cent to $3.67 and Chorus rallied 3.75 per cent to $8.02 on a strong day of trading for the sector.
The major two supermarkets also had a strong day, with Woolworths up 2.21 per cent at $36.48 and Coles closing 1.8 per cent higher at $22.61.
Vaccines company CSL closed 1.59 per cent higher at $99.47, ResMed jumped 1.3 per cent to $28 and Ramsay Health Care added 2.79 per cent to $37.64.
Offsetting an otherwise strong day of trading were the big iron ore producers, as the price of the commodity continued to slide.
Iron ore futures slid to $US101 a tonne, which in turn weighed on BHP as it fell 1.89 per cent to $60.08, Rio Tinto fell 1.81 per cent to $181.23 and Fortescue slumped 3.8 per cent to $19.75.
Markets were under pressure when they reopened following huge falls in the US and across Asia on Friday and Monday, including an 8 per cent slump on the South Korean Kospi.
But Asian stocks rallied on Tuesday as investors returned to the AI trade, while easing Middle East tensions also provided support and pushed oil prices down. Bargain buying had the Kospi leading gains, rebounding 8.2 per cent. Taipei added 2.8 per cent and Tokyo gained more than 2 per cent.
Hong Kong, Shanghai, Singapore, Manila, Mumbai, Bangkok and Wellington were also up.
IG market analyst Tony Sycamore said it was a remarkable recovery from a “stomach churning” 134-point fall at the open for the local bourse.
“The sharp early falls came as the ASX 200 responded to Friday night’s heavy losses on Wall Street, triggered by a robust non-farm payrolls report that pulled forward pricing for the first Fed rate hike into late 2026,” he said.
Markets turned after investors grew more confident that the latest ceasefire between Israel and Iran would hold after US President Donald Trump stepped in, urging both sides to “immediately stop shooting”. At the same time, weaker-than-expected local consumer and business confidence data added to rate-cut hopes in Australia.
Westpac’s consumer sentiment index fell 2.9 per cent in May to 80.6 – with a score of 100 considered optimistic – while the NAB Business Survey showed confidence improved slightly to -14 but remained weak across all industries.
“Some support emerged for the interest-rate sensitive consumer sectors after NAB chief economist Sally Auld said she no longer expects another RBA rate hike this cycle and believes the next move is likely lower,” Mr Sycamore said.
“This follows recent soft data, including last week’s tepid GDP print and today’s weak business and consumer confidence numbers, which suggest the economy has ‘lost momentum’.” In company news, James Hardie shares dropped 2.4 per cent to $31.32 after a shareholder class action alleges the building company misled investors over forecasted earnings. James Hardie denies the allegation and will “vigorously defend” the proceedings.
Ooh!media shares leapt 9.6 per cent to $1.37 after the business received a $765m takeover bid from Bain Capital. Zip Co was the biggest winner on Tuesday, closing up 5.88 per cent at $2.52, while Emerald Resources was the biggest laggard, down 9.03 per cent at $5.34.
